Document [0] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) At the onset of this pandemic, there was an urgency to mitigate this new viral illness with experimental therapies and drug repurposing. Since then, significant progress has been made in the management of COVID-19 due to the intense clinical research efforts globally that have resulted in novel therapeutics and vaccine development at an unprecedented speed. Currently, a variety of therapeutic options are available that include antiviral medications (e.g., molnupiravir, ritonavir in combination with nirmatrelvir, remdesivir), anti-SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibodies (e.g., bamlanivimab/etesevimab, casirivimab/imdevimab, sotrovimab, bebtelovimab), anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g., dexamethasone), immunomodulators agents (e.g., baricitinib, tocilizumab) are available under EUA for the management of COVID-19. [43]
Document [1] (Title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Function -- Antiviral Activity of Baricitinib) The potential antiviral activity of Baricitinib is embedded in the mechanism of viral cell entry of several viruses including SARS-CoV-2. The virus gains entry into the cells by a process known as clathrin-mediated endocytosis [12] . Clathrin is a protein that is found in abundance on the inner surface of the cell membrane, this protein is involved in making the vesicle-like covering around the viral particle leading to endocytosis/entry into the cell. The first step in the process involves the binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the host cell surface receptor / ACE 2. This leads to the activation of 2 kinases, the AP2-associated protein kinase 1 (AAK-1) as well as the cyclin D-associated kinase (GAK). These two kinases namely the AAK-1 and the GAK lead to the activation of host proteins called the adaptor protein complexes (APs). These APs recruit clathrin to form the cage-like structure around the virus leading to endocytosis. Baricitinib has been shown to inhibit AAK-1, and this has been the basis of the proposed antiviral action [13] . This mechanism of action was suggested by a London-based machine learning study (Benevolent) that evaluated a large repository of approved medications and found potential inhibitory activity at clinically achievable serum concentrations. Baricitinib uniquely showed a high affinity for AAK-1 in comparison to other JAK inhibitors like Tofacitinib and Upadacitinib [14] . It should be noted that SARS-CoV-2 utilizes several endocytotic pathways for viral entry and this benefit could easily be circumvented with viral utilization of an alternate endocytotic pathway [15] . Image 2 depicts the proposed antiviral action of Baricitinib.
Document [2] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors, such as acalabrutinib, ibrutinib, and rilzabrutinib, are tyrosine kinase inhibitors that regulate macrophage signaling and activation currently FDA-approved for some hematologic malignancies. It is proposed that macrophage activation occurs during the hyperinflammatory immune response seen in severe COVID-19. Results from a small off-label study of 19 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 who received acalabrutinib highlighted the potential clinical benefit of BTK inhibition. [119]  Clinical trials are in progress to validate the efficacy in severe COVID-19 illness.
Document [3] (Title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction -- The need for additional therapeutic agents) Over the course of the past year, our understanding of the pathophysiology of COVID-19 has improved. With ongoing randomized controlled trials evaluating therapies for COVID -19, like the RECOVERY Trial recruiting over 39,000 individuals, the guidelines for therapy for COVID-19 remain fluid. Despite a better understanding of the disease, the mortality and morbidity associated with COVID-19 remains unacceptably high.  The recent wave of overwhelming cases in the Republic of India is a grave example of the potential for disaster with COVID-19. There is a need for agents that can be added to the existing arsenal of approved medications. An ideal therapeutic agent should be reliable with robust clinical benefits. The recommendation for its use should be backed by evidence, and it should have a favorable and benign side effect profile. Apart from this, the agent should be readily available to the masses, should be inexpensive, and should be able to meet an exorbitant demand with an efficient supply chain. In an attempt to apply these criteria through this review; we evaluate the evidence for and against the concurrent use of Remdesivir and Baricitinib, indications and contraindications, mechanism of action, and the side effect profile of these drugs.
Document [4] (Title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Clinical Significance) Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir. This is an oral medication that consists of protease inhibitors. It has been shown to reduce hospitalization and death when given to high-risk, unvaccinated, non-hospitalized patients. It must be given within 5 days of symptoms onset. Of note, this medication has strong cytochrome P450 inhibition and therefore has many drug-drug interactions that need to be carefully assessed. Remdesivir. This is a nucleotide analog that inhibits the SARS-CoV-2 RNA polymerase. The recommended duration of therapy in this setting is 3 days. Molnupiravir. This is a mutagenic ribonucleoside antiviral agent. Due to the risk of genotoxicity with this agent, it is not recommended in pregnant patients. This agent should only be used if both of the above therapies are unavailable or cannot be given.

Document [6] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Lopinavir/ritonavir is FDA approved combo therapy for treating HIV and was proposed as antiviral therapy against COVID-19 during the early onset of the pandemic. Document [7] (Title: Casirivimab -- Indications) Casirivimab is a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against spike protein (S) of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) that causes coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). It is 1 of the SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibodies proposed for use in the clinical management of COVID-19. [1] On 21st November 2020, the combination of casirivimab and imdevimab was authorized for emergency use by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA). These are investigational drugs not approved for any specific indication. FDA allowed these drugs only to be administered together. Casirivimab (REGN10933) and imdevimab (REGN10987) are also known as REGN-COV2 or Regeneron. The combination drug has been shown to decrease viral load  and decrease the risk of hospitalization and emergency visits. REGN-COV2, administered prophylactically or therapeutically, has prevented COVID-19-induced sequelae in non-human primates. [1] [2]
Document [8] (Title: Imdevimab -- Indications) Imdevimab is a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against spike protein (S) of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) that causes Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). It is 1 of the SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibodies proposed for use in the clinical management of COVID-19. [1] On 21st November 2020, imdevimab and casirivimab cocktail were authorized for emergency use by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA). These are investigational drugs not approved for any specific indication. FDA allowed these drugs only to be administered together. Imdevimab (REGN10987), and casirivimab (REGN10933) is also known as REGN-COV2 or Regeneron. The combination drug has been shown to decrease viral load  and decrease the risk of hospitalization and emergency visits. It has also been shown to prevent virus-induced pathological sequelae when administered prophylactically or therapeutically in non-human primates. [1] [2]

Document [11] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Based on the NIH guidelines, individuals with mild illness can be managed in the ambulatory setting with supportive care and isolation. Laboratory and radiographic evaluations are routinely not indicated. Elderly patients and those with pre-existing conditions should be monitored closely until clinical recovery is achieved. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) Covid-19 treatment guidelines panel recommends the use of Paxlovid or Remdesivir in order of preference  as preferred therapies for outpatients at high risk of disease progression with a low threshold to consider hospitalization for closer monitoring. Document [12] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors Baricitinib is an oral selective inhibitor of Janus kinase (JAK) 1 and JAK 2 currently indicated for moderate to severely active rheumatoid arthritis patients. Baricitinib was considered a potential treatment for COVID-19 based on its inhibitory effect on SARS-CoV-2 endocytosis in vitro and on the intracellular signaling pathway of cytokines that cause the late-onset hyperinflammatory state that results in severe illness. [115] [116] This dual inhibitory effect makes it a promising therapeutic drug against all stages of COVID-19. A multicenter observational, retrospective study of 113 hospitalized patients with COVID-19 pneumonia who received baricitinib combined with lopinavir/ritonavir (baricitinib arm, n=113) or hydroxychloroquine and lopinavir/ritonavir (control arm, n=78) reported significant improvement in clinical symptoms and 2-week mortality rate in the baricitinib arm compared with the control arm. Results from the ACTT-2 trial, a double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating baricitinib plus remdesivir in hospitalized adult patients with COVID-19, reported that the combination therapy of baricitinib plus remdesivir was superior to remdesivir therapy alone in not only reducing recovery time but also accelerating clinical improvement in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, particularly who were receiving high flow oxygen supplementation or non-invasive ventilation. [117] Baricitinib, in combination with remdesivir, has been approved for clinical use in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 under a EUA issued by the FDA. The efficacy of baricitinib alone or in combination with remdesivir has not been evaluated in the SARS-CoV-2 variants, and there is limited data on the use of baricitinib with dexamethasone. Ruxolitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and 2 indicated for myeloproliferative disorders, polycythemia vera, and steroid-resistant GVHD. Similar to baricitinib, it has been hypothesized to have an inhibitory effect on cytokines' intracellular signaling pathway, making it a potential treatment against COVID-19. Results from a small prospective multicenter randomized controlled phase 2 trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of ruxolitinib reported no statistical difference from the standard of care. However, most patients demonstrated significant chest CT improvement and faster recovery from lymphopenia. [118] A large randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter trial (NCT04362137) is ongoing to assess ruxolitinib's efficacy and safety in patients with severe COVID-19. Tofacitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and JAK3 that is indicated for moderate to severe RA, psoriatic arthritis, and moderate to severe ulcerative colitis. Given its inhibitory effect on the inflammatory cascade, it was hypothesized that its use could ameliorate the viral inflammation-mediated lung injury in patients with severe COVID-19. Results from a small randomized controlled trial that evaluated the efficacy involving 289 patients who were randomized to receive tofacitinib or placebo showed that tofacitinib led to a lower risk of respiratory failure or death (PMID:34133856).

Document [16] (Title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Other Issues -- Limitations) The ACTT-2 study was well designed and provided valuable data, however, it was not powered to evaluate a mortality benefit associated with combination therapy of Remdesivir and Baricitinib. This proves to be a limitation in making this combination a standard of care. It will be interesting to see the results of the next phase of the Recovery trial, which will shed light on the concurrent use of steroids with Baricitinib.
Document [17] (Title: Imdevimab -- Mechanism of Action) Imdevimab is a recombinant neutralizing human immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal antibody to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. It binds to the S1 subunit of the spike protein receptor-binding domain (RBD), blocking the attachment of SARS-CoV-2 to the human ACE2 receptor. This prevents viral binding to the host cell, thereby preventing entry and replication of the virus, thus decreasing the viral load. Casirivimab binds to a non-overlapping portion of the spike protein RBD, similar to imdevimab, and the combination is theorized to limit the development of viral mutations. The cocktail of the 2 drugs was also found to have retained activity against the Alpha (B.1.1.7; UK), Beta (B.1.351; South Africa), Gamma (P.1; Brazil), Delta (B.1.617.2; India), Epsilon (B.1.427/429; California), Iota (B.1.526; New York), and Kappa (B.1.617.1; India) variants of SARS-CoV-2. The combination drug has no activity to the Omicron variant. [5] [7] [8]

Further studies are ongoing to establish the efficacy reported. [77] On 22 December 2021, the FDA issued a EUA authorizing the use of ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir for patients with mild to moderate COVID-19.

Document [22] (Title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Issues of Concern) Baricitinib has also been associated with a dose-dependent increased tendency to have venous as well as arterial thromboembolism, which is similar to other disease-modifying anti rheumatoid drugs (DMARD’s). It is for this reason that the FDA approved a 2 mg/day dose of Baricitinib for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, as the benefit of administering a 4 mg/day dose was unclear with an increased risk of thromboembolic events [23] . COVID-19 is associated with immune modulated hypercoagulability and whether the immunosuppressive effects of Baricitinib will act as a positive influence, or if its tendency to cause thromboembolic events will increase the risk of clots remains to be evaluated. The largest study to date with COVID-19 is ACTT-2 and the incidence of adverse events was noted to be less than the placebo arm which continues to be reassuring.

Document [28] (Title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management) Interleukin (IL)-1 Antagonists: Anakinra is an interleukin-1 receptor antagonist that is FDA-approved to treat rheumatoid arthritis. Its off-label use in severe COVID-19 was assessed in a small case-control study trial based on the rationale that severe COVID-19 is driven by cytokine production, including interleukin (IL)-1β. This trial revealed that of the 52 patients who received anakinra and 44 patients who received standard of care, anakinra reduced the need for invasive mechanical ventilation and mortality in patients with severe COVID-19. [107]  There is no data available regarding the efficacy of interleukin-1 receptor antagonists on the three new SARS-CoV-2 variants (B.1.1.7; B.1.351, and P.1). Given the insufficient data regarding this treatment based on case series only, this is not currently recommended to treat COVID-19 infection.

Beyond their antiviral activity, protease inhibitors share a broader pharmacologic relationship with compounds that act as inhibitory proteas, linking viral protease inhibition to proteasome inhibition pathways. Within the inhibitory proteas category, both hirudiny and ixazomib function as proteasome‑inhibiting agents, illustrating that they are types of inhibitory proteas. Remikiren is associated with the inhibitory proteas class through an inverse relationship, suggesting that it is not a direct member but rather a molecule whose activity is counter‑regulated or targeted by proteasome inhibition. This network of relationships underscores the overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition, providing insight into shared mechanisms that may influence drug selection, combination therapy, and potential repurposing strategies in patient care.

Document [9] (Title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129) Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.
Document [10] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 595) The NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology each categorize a set of pharmacologic agents as concepts relevant to COVID‑19. Both terminologies include kalcifediol, omeprazol, ritonavir, and crizanlizumab, indicating that these drugs have been considered in the context of COVID‑19 research or treatment. In addition, the terminologies incorporate the zinc compounds zinc sulfate (síran zinečnatý) and zinc gluconate, reflecting interest in zinc supplementation for immune support. Omeprazol is identified as a member of the benzimidazole chemical class, consistent with its structure as a benzimidazole‑derived proton‑pump inhibitor. Ritonavir, a protease inhibitor, is also grouped within the COVID‑19 agent subsets, highlighting its repurposing for antiviral therapy. Crizanlizumab is described in multiple ways: it belongs to the therapeutic class of immunological agents, is classified as an immunomodulator, and functions as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor. It is further characterized as both an immunomodulatory and an immunosuppressive product, underscoring its role in modulating vascular adhesion pathways, particularly in sickle cell disease, and its potential relevance to inflammatory processes in COVID‑19. Zinc supplements are represented as members of both zinc sulfate and zinc gluconate, indicating that these two zinc salts are common formulations used to provide elemental zinc for supplementation. Overall, the relationships illustrate how the NCIt COVID‑19 terminologies integrate a diverse set of agents—vitamin D metabolite (kalcifediol), a proton‑pump inhibitor (omeprazol), an antiretroviral protease inhibitor (ritonavir), a monoclonal antibody targeting P‑selectin (crizanlizumab), and zinc salts—reflecting a broad therapeutic landscape explored for COVID‑19 management and supportive care.

Document [12] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226) Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.
Document [13] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1221) Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Members of this class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is specifically linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibiting activity or be a precursor to such agents. The protease‑inhibitor class as a whole is also described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between viral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Compounds that are classified as inhibitory proteas include hirudiny and the approved proteasome inhibitor ixazomib. Remikiren is related to inhibitory proteas in an inverse hierarchical manner, implying that it represents a broader or distinct category that encompasses proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected pharmacologic landscape of viral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors, which can be relevant for designing combination therapies and understanding cross‑reactivity in patient care.
Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 358) Protease inhibitors that function as direct‑acting antivirals comprise fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir and tipranavir, linking these agents to the broader class of protease‑targeted antiviral therapy. Amprenavir is specifically associated with an “inhibitory proteas” entity, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibitory properties, and the entire class of protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, is described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Hirudiny is identified as an instance of inhibitory proteas, reinforcing its role as a proteasome‑inhibiting compound. The inhibitory proteas concept is further classified as a type of ixazomib, placing ixazomib within the proteasome‑inhibitor family, and it is also noted as a subclass of remikiren, indicating that remikiren represents a broader category that encompasses these proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors intersect, which can inform therapeutic strategies that target both viral replication and protein degradation pathways.
Document [15] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 237) The category “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron, all of which are classified within this ATC subgroup. This overlap illustrates that some agents within the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, such as ibalizumab, serve dual roles as both antiviral drugs and monoclonal antibody therapeutics, highlighting the convergence of antiviral pharmacotherapy and targeted biologic treatments in modern patient care.

Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 679) Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Key members of this class include the protease inhibitors ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to a downstream entity termed inhibitory proteas, reflecting a broader relationship between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. Inhibitory proteas is classified as ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. This connection highlights the pharmacologic overlap where agents originally developed as viral protease inhibitors share mechanistic features with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing both antiviral therapy and oncology drug development.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 619) Protease inhibitors that are classified as direct‑acting antivirals comprise several specific agents, including fosamprenavir, amprenavir and tipranavir. These antiviral protease inhibitors are closely linked to the broader category of proteasome‑inhibiting compounds (referred to as “inhibitory proteas”), indicating that their mechanism of action involves inhibition of proteasomal activity. Within this inhibitory proteas group, amprenavir is described as having a child relationship to the inhibitory proteas class, reinforcing its role as a proteasome‑targeting drug, and the protease‑inhibitor class as a whole is said to have a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas. Additional members of the inhibitory proteas family include the natural peptide hirudiny and the synthetic agent ixazomib, both of which are recognized as proteasome inhibitors. In terms of drug safety, amprenavir is contraindicated for use with cisapride monohydrate, meaning that co‑administration of these two agents should be avoided. Cisapride monohydrate itself is also listed as a contraindicated class for furosemide (furosemid), indicating that these medications should not be combined. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for preventing adverse drug interactions and ensuring safe patient care.
Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 418) The community’s knowledge base links several dermatologic, cardiovascular, antiviral, and metabolic agents through their pharmacologic properties, enzymatic interactions, and relevance to COVID‑19 therapeutics. The medicated shampoos marketed as chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL (VetraSeb) and a related chlorhexidine‑miconazole formulation contain miconazole as the active moiety; miconazole is metabolized by human cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) and acts as an inhibitor of CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes. Amiodarone (amiodaron) is also a CYP3A4 substrate, inhibits CYP2C9, CYP3A, and additional enzymes, and is contraindicated with ritonavir; it is used in cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation. Atenolol is similarly noted for treating cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation. Omeprazole (omeprazol) is a CYP3A4 substrate and enzyme inhibitor, and together with amiodarone, kalcifediol, and ritonavir it appears in the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, indicating a role in emerging COVID‑19 treatment frameworks. Zinc sulfate (síran zinečnatý) is also included in these COVID‑19 vocabularies, underscoring its therapeutic relevance. In the enzyme‑inhibition hierarchy, the class “inhibitory enzymů” (inhibitory enzymes) is a child of “inhibitory syntézy proteinů” (inhibitory protein synthesis) and is related to protein C; honokiol is classified as an instance of the inhibitory enzymes class, reflecting its function as an enzyme inhibitor. Collectively, these relationships emphasize the central role of CYP3A4 in drug metabolism, the potential for drug‑drug interactions when CYP2C9 or CYP3A inhibitors are co‑administered, and the intersection of dermatologic agents, cardiovascular drugs, antiviral therapies, and enzyme‑inhibition pathways in both routine patient care and COVID‑19‑related research.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1742) Maravirok, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab and tiloron are all classified as members of the “Other antivirals in ATC” group within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. This categorization indicates that each of these agents possesses antiviral activity but does not belong to the primary antiviral subclasses, placing them together as alternative or supplementary antiviral options. Recognizing these drugs as part of the “Other antivirals in ATC” category assists clinicians in expanding therapeutic choices for viral infections, especially when conventional antivirals are ineffective, contraindicated, or when a broader antiviral strategy is required.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096) Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, encompass several specific agents including ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. All of these drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral proteases, a broader relationship described as “inhibitory proteas.” Within this class, amprenavir is explicitly linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, underscoring its role in protease inhibition. The collective activity of these protease inhibitors as direct‑acting antivirals is central to antiviral treatment strategies, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where blocking viral protease function is essential for suppressing viral replication.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276) Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 58) The category “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises a group of antiviral agents that share a common classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical system. This group includes the fusion inhibitor enfuvirtide, the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc, the antiviral maribavir, the prodrug fostemsavir, the monoclonal antibody ibalizumab, and the antiviral moroxydine. All of these compounds are considered members of the “Other antivirals in ATC” class, reflecting their shared therapeutic purpose of treating viral infections. In addition to its antiviral role, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the broader class of monoclonal antibody therapeutics. Another monoclonal antibody, abciximab, is explicitly described as belonging to the monoclonal antibody class. Thus, both ibalizumab and abciximab exemplify the intersection between antiviral therapy and monoclonal antibody technology, highlighting the relevance of antibody‑based agents in the treatment of viral diseases.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825) Amprenavir is connected to an entity called inhibitory proteas, which is classified as ixazomib; thus, the inhibitory proteas component associated with amprenavir is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993) Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 0 -- Community 23) highlighted: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and pantoprazole is contraindicated with rilpivirine. Collectively, these relationships delineate the pharmacologic classifications, chemical families, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and formulation hierarchies of a diverse set of agents used in gastrointestinal, antiviral, antiretroviral, antifungal, and anticoagulant therapy.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745) Aspirin is recognized as a concept included in the NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, reflecting its relevance to COVID‑19–related research and therapeutic discussions. In addition, aspirin is noted to co‑occur with digoxin, indicating that these two medications are frequently mentioned together in clinical literature or practice, which may highlight considerations of combined use, potential drug interactions, or shared therapeutic contexts.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310) Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 673) Neuraminidase inhibitors are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, meaning they target viral enzymes essential for replication. Zanamivir is a specific member of the neuraminidase inhibitor class and therefore also functions as a direct‑acting antiviral.
